title: Shipping Instructions for Sources
narrationScript: The moment a loaded camera leaves the job site, you stop being just a radiographer and become a hazmat shipper, answerable to two rulebooks at once. NRC rules in 10 CFR Part 71 certify the package, and DOT rules in 49 CFR govern the move. A working iridium-192 source exceeds its A-one value of 27 curies, so the loaded exposure device travels as an NRC-certified Type B package, configured exactly as its certificate requires. Locked, plugged, and surveyed. That survey decides everything else. The highest reading at one meter, rounded up to the next tenth, becomes the transport index, and together with the surface reading it selects the label category. A Yellow Three package puts RADIOACTIVE placards on all four sides of the vehicle. The shipping papers carry the UN number, the nuclide, special form, the activity, the label category, the transport index, the certificate ID, and a twenty-four hour emergency number, and they ride within the driver's reach. Block and brace the package, keep it under constant surveillance, and deliver it by procedure.

sections.heading: Two Rulebooks Govern Every Mile
sections.body:
The moment a licensed sealed source leaves the site — whether it is handed to a freight carrier or rides in your own crew truck to the next job — it becomes a regulated shipment. **10 CFR 71.5** makes the connection explicit: a licensee who transports licensed material off-site, or delivers it to a carrier for transport, must comply with the DOT Hazardous Materials Regulations in **49 CFR Parts 171–180**. There is no short-trip exception and no company-truck exception.

The two agencies divide the work:

- **NRC (10 CFR Part 71)** approves and certifies the *package* — the engineered container that keeps the source shielded and contained through an accident — and sets quality assurance and transport security requirements for licensees.
- **DOT (49 CFR)** governs the *shipment* — classification, package selection, marking, labeling, shipping papers, emergency information, placarding, vehicle securement, and training.

That training requirement has teeth. Anyone who prepares, offers, or transports hazardous material is a **hazmat employee** under **49 CFR 172.704** and must complete general awareness, function-specific, safety, and security awareness training before performing those duties unsupervised, with recurrent training at least every **three years**. An IRRSP card is not hazmat training — they are separate obligations, and a radiographer who signs shipping papers without it puts the company in violation before the truck leaves the yard.

sections.heading: Special Form, A1, and Why the Camera Is a Type B Package
sections.body:
A radiography source is a **special form** capsule — a sealed, high-integrity container that has passed impact, percussion, bending, and heat tests (49 CFR 173.469) without leaking. Special form matters because the transport hazard is then *external radiation* rather than loose contamination, and the regulations reward that with higher activity allowances.

For every radionuclide, **49 CFR 173.435** lists an **A1 value** — the maximum activity of special form material permitted in a **Type A package** (A2 is the lower limit for normal form material). The numbers a radiographer should know:

- **Ir-192: A1 = 1 TBq (27 Ci)**
- **Co-60: A1 = 0.4 TBq (10.8 Ci)**

A **Type A** package (DOT Specification 7A, shipped as *UN3332, Radioactive material, Type A package, special form, non fissile or fissile-excepted*) only has to survive **normal** transport — rain, a four-foot drop, stacking, and a penetration bar. Compare the A1 values with a working source, commonly around 100 Ci of Ir-192, and the conclusion is automatic: **a loaded exposure device or source changer exceeds A1 and must move as a Type B package**, shipped as *UN2916, Radioactive material, Type B(U) package, non fissile or fissile-excepted*.

A **Type B** design is certified to survive the **hypothetical accident sequence** — a 30-foot drop onto an unyielding surface, a 40-inch drop onto a steel puncture bar, a fully engulfing 1,475 °F fire for 30 minutes, and water immersion — with its shielding and containment intact. Your exposure device *is* the certified package, listed in an NRC **Certificate of Compliance (CoC)**. Shipping under the general license of **10 CFR 71.17** obligates you to be a **registered user** of that certificate, keep a current copy, operate under an approved quality assurance program, and configure the device for transport **exactly as the certificate conditions require** — source locked in the shielded position, safety plug and covers installed.

sections.caption: A loaded gamma exposure device prepared for transport as a Type B(U) package, with the required markings and labels called out.

sections.heading: Survey First — the Label Writes Itself
sections.body:
No package is ever labeled from memory or from paperwork. Labeling starts with a **radiation survey** of the finished, locked package using a calibrated instrument, and it produces two numbers:

- the **maximum reading at the package surface**, and
- the **maximum reading at one meter** from the surface.

The one-meter number, expressed in mrem/h and **rounded up to the next tenth**, is the **transport index (TI)**. Measure 2.28 mrem/h and the TI is 2.3; measure 2.62 and it is 2.7. The TI rides on the label and the shipping papers, limits what a vehicle may carry, and tells every handler downstream how much radiation the package projects.

Surface reading and TI together select the label category — and the package must satisfy **both** criteria of a category, or it moves up to the next one. A package reading 40 mrem/h at the surface (within Yellow-II) but carrying a TI of 1.4 (over 1.0) ships as **Yellow-III**.

Completing the label: enter the **contents** (the radionuclide — Ir-192), the **activity in SI units** (for example 3.7 TBq, with curies optional in parentheses), and, on Yellow labels, the **TI**. Apply **two labels, on opposite sides** of the package. The ceilings for routine, non-exclusive-use transport are **200 mrem/h at the surface, a TI of 10 per package, and a TI total of 50 per vehicle** — beyond those numbers a shipment moves only under exclusive-use controls (49 CFR 173.441(b)).

sections.caption: Radioactive material label categories — a package must satisfy every criterion of a category, or it moves up to the next one.
rows: 0 (a measured TI of 0.05 or less may be rounded to zero)
No placard required by this label
No placard required by this label; TI counts toward the vehicle total
RADIOACTIVE placards on each side and each end — any quantity
Over 200 mrem/h (up to 1,000 mrem/h in a closed exclusive-use vehicle)
Exclusive use only, under 49 CFR 173.441(b) controls

sections.caption: Label category criteria and the transport index: the highest one-meter reading, rounded up to the next tenth, becomes the TI.

sections.heading: Shipping Papers: The Shipment's Voice
sections.body:
The shipping paper describes the shipment to carriers, to inspectors, and — after an accident — to emergency responders. The **basic description** appears in a prescribed order: **ID number, proper shipping name, hazard class**. For a loaded camera:

**UN2916, Radioactive material, Type B(U) package, non fissile or fissile-excepted, 7**

Class 7 then requires additional entries (49 CFR 172.203(d)):

- the **radionuclide** — Ir-192
- the **physical and chemical form** — "special form"
- the **activity in SI units** — 3.0 TBq (81 Ci)
- the **label category** — RADIOACTIVE YELLOW-III
- the **transport index** — 2.3
- the **package certificate ID** — for example, USA/9296/B(U)-96

Add an **emergency response telephone number monitored 24 hours a day** (49 CFR 172.604) and keep **emergency response information** with the paper (49 CFR 172.602). The shipper signs the **certification** — "This is to certify that the above-named materials are properly classified, described, packaged, marked and labeled…" — when offering the shipment to a carrier; a company hauling its own sources as a **private motor carrier** is excepted from the signed certification, and from nothing else.

On the road, the papers ride **within the driver's immediate reach with the seat belt fastened**, and go on the **driver's seat or in the driver's door pouch** whenever the driver leaves the cab (49 CFR 177.817(e)).

sections.title: The cab is the first place responders look
sections.body: If your truck is hit and you are unconscious, three things still speak for the shipment: the **placards** on the vehicle, the **shipping papers** in the cab, and the **24-hour emergency number** on them. That is why the reach rule is absolute — papers within arm's reach while you drive, on the seat or in the door pouch when you step away. A perfect package with the papers buried in the lockbox fails at the one moment the whole system was built for.

sections.heading: Placards, Securement, and the Final Walk-Around
sections.body:
**Placards.** A vehicle carrying any package labeled **RADIOACTIVE YELLOW-III** displays the RADIOACTIVE placard on **each side and each end — four placards, for any quantity** (49 CFR 172.504, Table 1). Packages labeled White-I or Yellow-II do not trigger placards. Most loaded exposure devices survey out as Yellow-III, which is why radiography trucks run placarded.

**Securement.** Class 7 packages must be **blocked and braced** so they cannot shift during normal transport (49 CFR 177.842(d)) — in practice, locked inside a secured lockbox fixed to the truck. Observe the separation distances of 49 CFR 177.842(b), keeping packages away from occupied positions and undeveloped film according to TI.

**Security.** A typical radiography source exceeds the **category 2 threshold for Ir-192 — 0.8 TBq (about 22 Ci)** — so **10 CFR Part 37, Subpart D** applies in transit: verify the recipient's license before transfer, use carriers that provide package tracking and constant control, and maintain **constant control and/or surveillance** when transporting your own material.

**The walk-around before the wheels roll:**

- source locked, safety plug and cover installed per the CoC
- survey done, TI computed, two correct labels applied on opposite sides
- markings legible: UN number and proper shipping name, TYPE B(U), certificate ID, gross weight, consignor or consignee
- papers complete, signed as required, in the cab, emergency number live
- placards on all four sides, clean and visible
- package blocked, braced, and locked; vehicle secured

A release, suspected leakage, or package damage on the road is reportable — immediate notice under **49 CFR 171.15** plus your license's reporting procedures. Secure the scene, establish distance, and get the RSO on the phone before anything else moves.

figures.title: Type B(U) package markings on a loaded exposure device
Label categories and the transport index

interactive.title: Ship the Camera to the Next Job
interactive.intro: Your crew finishes a job with an 81-curie (3.0 TBq) Ir-192 source, and dispatch sends you 120 highway miles to the next site. You are a trained hazmat employee, and the camera is an NRC-certified Type B(U) package. Prepare the shipment the way the regulations demand.

steps.prompt: The source is cranked back and the device sits in front of you. What comes first in preparing it for the road?
options: Load it into the truck lockbox right away — the post-exposure survey already proved it safe
Confirm the source is locked in the shielded position with the safety plug and cover installed per the certificate, then survey the package — maximum surface reading and maximum reading at one meter
Wrap the device in padding so the labels stay clean, then load it
Remove the old labels so the package travels unmarked until the office relabels it
steps.feedback: Certificate configuration comes first — locked, plugged, covered — and then the two survey numbers that everything else depends on: label category, TI, papers, and placards. A package never gets its label from memory; it gets it from a survey.

steps.prompt: Your survey: maximum 96 mrem/h at the surface, maximum 2.28 mrem/h at one meter. What goes on the package?
options: One Yellow-II label with TI 2.3 — the surface reading is under 200 mrem/h
Two Yellow-III labels with TI 2.2 — round the one-meter reading to the nearest tenth
Two RADIOACTIVE YELLOW-III labels on opposite sides — contents Ir-192, activity 3.0 TBq (81 Ci), TI 2.3
A White-I label — the device is shielded, so the external readings do not count
steps.feedback: The TI is 2.28 rounded UP to 2.3 — never to the nearest tenth. A surface reading of 96 mrem/h and a TI above 1.0 both demand Yellow-III, and the category's criteria must all be satisfied. Two labels go on opposite sides, with contents, activity in SI units, and the TI filled in.

steps.prompt: Now the shipping paper. Which basic description is correct?
options: UN2916, Radioactive material, Type B(U) package, non fissile or fissile-excepted, 7 — followed by Ir-192, special form, 3.0 TBq (81 Ci), RADIOACTIVE YELLOW-III, TI 2.3, and the package certificate ID
Radioactive camera, 81 curies, handle with care — plus the office phone number
UN3332, Radioactive material, Type A package, special form, non fissile or fissile-excepted, 7 — Type A is correct because the source is special form
Class 7, Radioactive material, UN2916 — the order does not matter as long as everything appears
steps.feedback: The sequence is prescribed: ID number, proper shipping name, hazard class — then the Class 7 additions: radionuclide, form, activity in SI units, label category, TI, and certificate ID. UN3332 is illegal here because 81 Ci exceeds the Ir-192 A1 of 27 Ci. Add the 24-hour emergency number, and sign the certification if you are offering the package to a carrier.

steps.prompt: Last steps before the wheels roll?
options: Placard the rear of the truck and keep the papers in the lockbox with the camera
Skip the placards — 120 miles is a local move under the same license
RADIOACTIVE placards on each side and each end, the device blocked and braced in the secured lockbox, papers within reach in the cab, and constant control and surveillance for the whole trip
Placard all four sides only if the route crosses a state line
steps.feedback: A Yellow-III package means four placards — any quantity, any distance, any route. Block and brace per 49 CFR 177.842, keep the papers within the driver's reach per 177.817(e), and because 81 Ci of Ir-192 is a category 2 quantity, 10 CFR Part 37 requires constant control and surveillance in transit.

checkQuestions.question: Your highest reading one meter from the surface of a finished package is 2.62 mrem/h. What transport index goes on the label?
checkQuestions.explanation: The TI is the maximum one-meter reading in mrem/h, rounded UP to the next tenth — 2.62 becomes 2.7. Rounding down or to the nearest tenth understates the package's radiation to every handler downstream.

checkQuestions.question: A package reads a maximum of 40 mrem/h at the surface and 1.4 mrem/h at one meter. Which label does it carry?
options: Yellow-II, because the surface reading is within the Yellow-II limit of 50 mrem/h
Yellow-III, because the TI exceeds 1.0 and a package must satisfy every criterion of a category
White-I, because both readings are low
No label — readings this low are exempt from labeling
checkQuestions.explanation: Label category is set by surface reading AND transport index together. A TI of 1.4 exceeds the Yellow-II ceiling of 1.0, so the package moves up to Yellow-III even though its surface reading alone would fit Yellow-II.

checkQuestions.question: One package labeled RADIOACTIVE YELLOW-III is loaded on your truck. What placarding does the vehicle require?
options: None — placards apply only to shipments over 100 curies
Placards only if the total activity exceeds the A1 value
One RADIOACTIVE placard on the rear of the vehicle
RADIOACTIVE placards on each side and each end — required for any quantity bearing a Yellow-III label
checkQuestions.explanation: Yellow-III labeled material is a Table 1 hazard under 49 CFR 172.504: the RADIOACTIVE placard goes on each side and each end of the vehicle regardless of quantity. White-I and Yellow-II packages do not trigger placards.

checkQuestions.question: Why does a loaded exposure device containing 100 Ci of Ir-192 ship as a Type B package?
options: 100 Ci exceeds the Ir-192 A1 value of 1 TBq (27 Ci), and special form activity beyond A1 must move in an accident-tested, certified Type B design
DOT requires Type B packaging for all sealed sources regardless of activity
Type A packages are not permitted to contain special form material
Yellow-III labels may only be applied to Type B packages
checkQuestions.explanation: A1 is the most special form activity a Type A package may carry — 1 TBq (27 Ci) for Ir-192. A 100 Ci working source far exceeds it, so the move requires a Type B package certified against the hypothetical accident sequence: drop, puncture, fire, and immersion.

checkQuestions.question: While you drive a source shipment, where must the shipping papers be?
options: Locked in the lockbox with the exposure device
On file at the office with the RSO
Within your immediate reach with your seat belt fastened — and on the driver's seat or in the driver's door pouch whenever you leave the cab
Anywhere in the vehicle, as long as you can describe their location
checkQuestions.explanation: 49 CFR 177.817(e) puts the papers within the driver's immediate reach while belted, and on the seat or in the door pouch when the driver is out — so emergency responders can find them even if the driver cannot answer.

references: CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §11.3 (shipping instructions — sources)
10 CFR Part 71 — Packaging and Transportation of Radioactive Material (§71.5 DOT compliance; §71.17 general license for NRC-approved packages)
49 CFR Parts 171–178 — DOT Hazardous Materials Regulations (§172.403 labels; §§172.200–204 shipping papers; §172.504 placarding; §173.441 radiation limits; §§177.817, 177.842 highway carriage)
10 CFR Part 37, Subpart D — Physical Protection of Category 1 and Category 2 Quantities of Radioactive Material in Transit
